content:
# Genesis Chapter 3 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 2v–3r | **Source Image:** source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_003_page_001.jpg, GEN_003_page_002.jpg
**Additional Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209) — LXX | **View Vaticanus online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209

---

**1** Now the serpent was more crafty than any beast of the field that the LORD God had made. And he said to the woman, "Is it indeed so that God has said, 'You shall not eat from any tree of the garden'?"

**2** And the woman said to the serpent, "From the fruit of the trees of the garden we may eat.

**3** But from the fruit of the tree that is in the midst of the garden, God has said, 'You shall not eat from it, and you shall not touch it, lest you die.'"

**4** And the serpent said to the woman, "You shall not surely die.

**5** For God knows that in the day you eat from it, your eyes shall be opened, and you shall be as God, knowing good and evil."

**6** And the woman saw that the tree was good for food, and that it was a delight to the eyes, and that the tree was desirable to make one wise. And she took from its fruit and ate, and she gave also to her husband with her, and he ate.

**7** And the eyes of them both were opened, and they knew that they were naked. And they sewed fig leaves together and made coverings for themselves.

**8** And they heard the sound of the LORD God walking in the garden in the wind of the day, and the man and his wife hid themselves from the presence of the LORD God among the trees of the garden.

**9** And the LORD God called to the man, and He said to him, "Where are you?"

**10** And he said, "I heard your sound in the garden, and I was afraid because I was naked, and I hid myself."

**11** And He said, "Who told you that you were naked? Have you eaten from the tree from which I commanded you not to eat?"

**12** And the man said, "The woman whom You gave to be with me — she gave me from the tree, and I ate."

**13** And the LORD God said to the woman, "What is this that you have done?" And the woman said, "The serpent deceived me, and I ate."

**14** And the LORD God said to the serpent, "Because you have done this, cursed are you above all the livestock and above every beast of the field. On your belly you shall go, and dust you shall eat all the days of your life.

**15** And I will put enmity between you and the woman, and between your seed and her seed. He shall bruise your head, and you shall bruise his heel."

**16** To the woman He said, "I will greatly multiply your pain in your childbearing. In pain you shall bring forth children. And toward your husband shall be your desire, and he shall rule over you."

**17** And to the man He said, "Because you have listened to the voice of your wife, and have eaten from the tree of which I commanded you, saying, 'You shall not eat from it' — cursed is the ground on your account. In painful toil you shall eat from it all the days of your life.

**18** And thorns and thistles it shall bring forth for you, and you shall eat the plant of the field.

**19** By the sweat of your face you shall eat bread, until you return to the ground, for from it you were taken. For you are dust, and to dust you shall return."

**20** And the man called his wife's name Eve, for she was the mother of all living.

**21** And the LORD God made garments of skin for the man and for his wife, and He clothed them.

**22** And the LORD God said, "Behold, the man has become as one of Us, to know good and evil. And now, lest he stretch out his hand and take also from the tree of life, and eat, and live forever —"

**23** So the LORD God sent him out from the garden of Eden, to work the ground from which he was taken.

**24** He drove out the man, and He placed at the east of the garden of Eden the cherubim and the flame of a sword turning itself, to guard the way to the tree of life.

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

### Source Manuscripts
This translation was produced from direct reading of two manuscript image sources:

1. **Leningrad Codex (Codex Leningradensis, B19A)**, dated c. 1008 CE. West Semitic Research Project photographs by Bruce E. Zuckerman. Genesis 3 spans Folios 2v–3r. The three-column format with full Tiberian vocalization and cantillation is maintained throughout, consistent with Genesis 1–2. Both folios were read directly; the text is clearly legible throughout, including the divine name יהוה, the marginal Masorah parva notations, and the parashah markers.

2. **Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209)** — Septuagint/LXX, 4th century CE. The Genesis 3 page (viewed at https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209) preserves the chapter in the characteristic uncial hand of Vaticanus, written in three columns per page in scriptio continua, without word spaces or verse divisions. The page is headed ΓΑΡ. ΙΙΙ (CAP. III). The Greek is legible throughout, including the key variant readings noted below.

**Additional reference:** Dead Sea Scrolls 4QGen-b (consulted for this chapter where it bears on text-critical questions).

---

### Key Translation Decisions

**1. עָרוּם (arum) — "crafty"** (v. 1)

The serpent is described as עָרוּם (arum), an adjective from the root ערם. The same root in its plural form עֲרוּמִּים (arummim) was used at the end of chapter 2 (v. 25) to describe the man and woman as "naked." This is one of the most deliberate wordplays in the Hebrew Bible: the chapter closes with הָאָדָם וְאִשְׁתּוֹ עֲרוּמִּים ("the man and his wife were naked") and opens with הַנָּחָשׁ הָיָה עָרוּם ("the serpent was crafty"). The forms are vocalized differently by the Masoretes — arummim (naked) and arum (crafty/shrewd) — but the consonantal text is identical. The English reader cannot hear this, but it is preserved in the translation notes: innocence and cunning share a single consonantal body in the Hebrew.

"Crafty" is preferred over "subtle" (KJV), "shrewd" (NASB), or "clever." It carries the right combination of intelligence and moral ambiguity: the serpent is not merely clever, but uses cleverness for a particular purpose. The LXX renders עָרוּם here as φρονιμώτατος — "most prudent / most sensible of all" — which is striking: the very word used in Greek wisdom literature (φρόνιμος) for the person of practical wisdom. The LXX serpent is, in Greek ethical terms, the most practically wise of all creatures. This is a deeply ironic rendering that deepens rather than diminishes the Hebrew.

**2. הַנָּחָשׁ (hannachash) — "the serpent"** (throughout)

The creature is definitively identified as הַנָּחָשׁ (hannachash), "the serpent" — a specific, named creature, an animal, not a supernatural figure. The text introduces him simply as an animal, one of the beasts of the field that the LORD God had made. The identification of the serpent with the Adversary (הַשָּׂטָן, ha-satan) or with the devil is a later interpretive tradition; the text itself makes no such identification. This translation renders הַנָּחָשׁ throughout as "the serpent" without editorial comment on its ultimate identity, following the literal scope of the Hebrew. The theological tradition of identification is noted here, not imported into the text.

**3. אַף כִּי (af ki) — "Is it indeed so that"** (v. 1)

The serpent's opening question begins with אַף כִּי, an interrogative construction that introduces a tone of incredulity or exaggeration. The traditional rendering "Has God really said?" (NASB, ESV) captures the skeptical tone but flattens the rhetorical structure. The Hebrew form implies a kind of intensified surprise: "Can it really be that God has said...?" or "Is it so — that God has said...?" I render "Is it indeed so that" to preserve the structure of genuine questioning while retaining the note of incredulity. The serpent does not deny outright; he interrogates, inviting the woman to question whether the command was as strict as she remembers it.

Note also: the serpent slightly misquotes the command. God said "from every tree of the garden you may freely eat" (2:16) with one exception. The serpent frames it as a total prohibition — "You shall not eat from any tree?" — which is an overstatement designed to elicit a correction. The woman's correction in vv. 2–3 is accurate, but she adds something God did not say: "and you shall not touch it." This addition is not in the MT of 2:17. Whether the woman is making the prohibition stricter (a fence around the Torah), or whether she is reporting an oral tradition not recorded in the narrative, the text leaves open.

**4. לֹא מוֹת תְּמֻתוּן (lo mot tamutun) — "You shall not surely die"** (v. 4)

The serpent's contradiction of God's command in 2:17. God said מוֹת תָּמוּת (mot tamut), "you shall surely die" — the Hebrew infinitive absolute construction for emphasis: "dying you shall die." The serpent negates this with לֹא מוֹת תְּמֻתוּן (lo mot tamutun), "not dying shall you die" — again with the infinitive absolute, but negated. The parallel is exact: the serpent is not merely asserting that they will survive; he is structurally negating the very grammatical form of God's prohibition. This is preserved in the formal rendering by retaining "surely" as the marker of the emphatic construction: "You shall not surely die" (rather than "You will not die") — though English does not reproduce the doubled verb.

**5. וִהְיִיתֶם כֵּאלֹהִים יֹדְעֵי טוֹב וָרָע (vihyitem ke-elohim yodei tov vara) — "you shall be as God, knowing good and evil"** (v. 5)

The phrase כֵּאלֹהִים (ke-elohim) means "like God" or "like gods" — the Hebrew is ambiguous. אֱלֹהִים is morphologically plural and is the word used both for the God of Israel (singular referent) and for divine beings or gods generally. The LXX renders it ὡς θεοί ("as gods"), taking the plural: "you shall be as gods, knowing good and evil." This is an ancient and defensible reading of the ambiguity.

I translate "as God" rather than "as gods," following the consistent pattern of this translation in which אֱלֹהִים in narrative contexts where the God of Israel is in view is rendered as the singular "God." The same ambiguity returns at v. 22, where the LORD God says "the man has become as one of Us, to know good and evil" — the plural "Us" (אַחַד מִמֶּנּוּ, "one of Us") there suggesting a divine council. The LXX variant ("as gods") is noted as a live alternative; the MT is followed with the singular.

**6. כִּי עֵירֻמִּם הֵם (ki eyrummim hem) — "that they were naked"** (v. 7)

The word for "naked" here (עֵירֻמִּים, eyrummim) is from the root ערה, distinct in form from the עֲרוּמִּים of 2:25 (though related in etymology). The Masoretic pointing distinguishes them: the nakedness of innocence (arum/arummim in 2:25, the same root as "crafty") and the nakedness of shame (eyrummim in 3:7) use overlapping but not identical forms. The play of these roots through chapters 2–3 is intricate: "crafty" (3:1), "naked" (2:25), and "naked in shame" (3:7) are all phonologically proximate in the Hebrew, and the Masoretes' vocalization preserves the distinctions while keeping the echo alive.

**7. קוֹל יְהוָה אֱלֹהִים מִתְהַלֵּךְ (qol YHWH elohim mithallek) — "the sound of the LORD God walking"** (v. 8)

The word קוֹל (qol) can mean "voice" or "sound" — both renderings are ancient. The LXX reads τὴν φωνὴν κυρίου τοῦ θεοῦ, using φωνή ("voice" or "sound"), which is similarly ambiguous. The context is the wind of the afternoon: what the man and woman hear may be the rustling sound of the LORD God's approach through the garden in the breeze, or it may be His voice. The formal equivalence approach preserves the ambiguity: "the sound of the LORD God walking in the garden" allows both interpretations. "The voice of the LORD God" (KJV, NKJV) is also within range and is the more traditional rendering; I prefer "sound" to keep the sensory image of presence — footsteps, rustling, movement — distinct from the directly verbal connotation of "voice."

לְרוּחַ הַיּוֹם (leruach hayyom) — "in the wind of the day" / "in the cool of the day." רוּחַ (ruach) here is "wind / breath / spirit" — the word is the same in all three senses. The traditional "cool of the day" (KJV) interprets רוּחַ as the cool wind of the late afternoon, which is meteorologically accurate to the climate of the Levant. I render "the wind of the day" to preserve the literal value of ruach rather than importing the interpretive "cool." The LXX renders it with τὸ δειλινόν ("in the evening," literally "the afternoon/evening time"), which is a temporal interpretation rather than a meteorological one. The MT's ruach hayyom is neither purely temporal nor purely meteorological; it is the moment when the wind moves through the garden.

**8. וְאֵיפֹה אַתָּה (ayekkah) — "Where are you?"** (v. 9)

The divine question is a single word in Hebrew: אַיֶּכָּה (ayekkah), "where-are-you?" It is not a question of omniscient ignorance — the LORD God does not fail to know where the man is. It is an invitation to self-disclosure, a judicial summons, the first instance of divine interrogation in the Bible. It is rendered in three words in English ("Where are you?") as the language requires, but the Hebrew reader hears a single, weighty monosyllabic-inflected word. The Targum Onkelos renders it with the equivalent Aramaic construction.

**9. הַצֶּאֱלִים (hatsela'elim) — fig leaves** (v. 7); חֲגֹרֹת (chagorot) — "coverings"

חֲגֹרֹת is from the root חגר, "to gird, to bind on." The word means specifically a belt, girdle, or loincloth — something tied or bound around the waist. I render "coverings" as a slightly broader term that preserves the function without over-specifying. The LXX renders it περιζώματα ("loincloths / aprons tied around the waist"), confirming the sense of girded wrappings rather than full garments. The fig leaves are stitched together, the verb תָּפַר (tafar) meaning "to sew, to stitch." This is precise: it is sewn clothing, not merely draped material.

**10. הוּא יְשׁוּפְךָ רֹאשׁ (hu yeshufekha rosh) — "He shall bruise your head"** (v. 15)

This verse, known in the Latin tradition as the Protoevangelium ("first gospel"), is one of the most interpretively contested sentences in the entire Hebrew Bible.

The verb שׁוּף (shuf) appears only three times in the Hebrew Bible (Gen 3:15 twice; Job 9:17), and its precise meaning is disputed. The traditional rendering "bruise" (KJV, RSV) is conservative. Other proposals include "crush" (ESV, NIV), "strike" (NASB), and "lie in wait for" (a less-attested reading). The LXX renders both instances with τηρήσει ("he shall watch for / he shall guard against"), which is an interpretive flattening: it avoids the combat imagery and renders the conflict as a mutual watching or lying-in-wait. The Vulgate famously renders the first instance as ipsa conteret ("she shall crush") — referring the pronoun to the woman rather than the seed — a reading that has been enormously influential in Marian interpretation but which departs from the Hebrew masculine הוּא (hu, "he"). The MT is clear: the masculine pronoun refers to the woman's seed.

I render both instances of שׁוּף with "bruise" — the standard formal rendering — to preserve the symmetry: "He shall bruise your head, and you shall bruise his heel." Both actions use the same Hebrew verb; the asymmetry is in the body part (head vs. heel), not the verb. "Crush" is defensible for the first instance (a blow to the head is more lethal than a bite to the heel) but introduces an asymmetry not in the text. "Bruise" applied to both preserves the formal parallelism while acknowledging that the blows are not equivalent in effect.

**11. עֶצֶב (etsev) — "pain" / "painful toil"** (vv. 16–17)

The noun עֶצֶב (etsev, vv. 16 and 17) and the related עִצָּבוֹן (itsavon, also v. 16) are rendered "pain" and "painful toil" respectively. Both come from the root עצב, which denotes painful labor, grief, and toil together — it is the word of effort that wounds. In v. 16 it is applied to childbearing (עִצָּבוֹן, itsavon): the pain of the woman's labor. In v. 17 the same root appears for the man's agricultural labor (בְּעִצָּבוֹן, be-itsavon): painful toil in the field. The use of the same root for both judgments is deliberate symmetry — both the woman's domain and the man's domain are now marked by the same quality of painful effort. I preserve this link by rendering both with the "pain" stem: "pain in your childbearing" (v. 16) and "in painful toil you shall eat from it" (v. 17).

**12. תְּשׁוּקָה (teshuqah) — "desire"** (v. 16)

וְאֶל אִישֵׁךְ תְּשׁוּקָתֵךְ (ve-el ishekh teshuqatekh) — "and toward your husband shall be your desire." The word תְּשׁוּקָה (teshuqah) occurs only three times in the Hebrew Bible: here (Gen 3:16), in the Song of Songs 7:11 (where the beloved says "his desire is for me"), and in Genesis 4:7 (where sin's desire is for Cain). It is a strong, directional longing — desire oriented toward something or someone. Its precise sense in this verse is contested: does it mean longing, desire, or a drive toward control? The context of the whole clause is that this desire exists alongside — or in tension with — the man's rule over the woman, a condition presented here as a consequence of the fall rather than the original design (cf. 2:18–23, where no hierarchy is explicit). I translate "desire" as the most direct rendering of teshuqah, noting that the relational dynamic described is part of the post-fall condition, not the creational ideal.

**13. חַוָּה (Chavvah) — "Eve"** (v. 20)

The man names his wife חַוָּה (Chavvah), from the root חיה, "to live." The explanation follows: כִּי הִוא הָיְתָה אֵם כָּל חָי (ki hi hayetah em kol chay), "for she was the mother of all living." The name Eve is the traditional English form of Chavvah via the Latin Heva. The Hebrew root connection between the name and the phrase "all living" is explicit: Chavvah / chay — the one whose name holds life is the source of all living. The LXX renders the name as Ζωή (Zoe), the Greek word for "life," which is an interpretive translation of the name itself rather than a transliteration. "Zoe, mother of all living" makes the etymology visible in Greek in a way that "Chavvah" does not. The MT is followed with "Eve" as the established English transliteration.

**14. כָּתְנוֹת עוֹר (kotnot or) — "garments of skin"** (v. 21)

The LORD God makes for the man and his wife כָּתְנוֹת עוֹר (kotnot or), "garments of skin." The word כְּתֹנֶת (ketonet) is the word for a tunic or long garment — the same word used of Joseph's tunic of many colors (Gen 37:3). עוֹר (or) is skin, hide, leather. These are substantial garments — not fig-leaf aprons (חֲגֹרֹת) but full tunics of animal hide. The LXX renders χιτῶνας δερματίνους ("leather tunics"), the χιτών being the standard Greek undertunic worn next to the body. The replacement of sewn fig-leaf coverings with divine provision of leather garments is theologically resonant: the first blood, the first death, the first act of divine clothing. These implications are not explicit in the text and are not imported into the translation; the words are rendered literally and allowed to carry their own weight.

**15. הַכְּרֻבִים (hakkeruvim) — "the cherubim"** (v. 24)

The LORD God places הַכְּרֻבִים (hakkeruvim) — the cherubim — east of Eden, together with לַהַט הַחֶרֶב הַמִּתְהַפֶּכֶת (lahat hacherv hamithafekkhet), "the flame of a sword turning itself." The cherubim are not described here — no anatomy, no wings, no wheels. They are placed as guardians. The turning or revolving sword-flame (the Hithpael participle of הפך, "to turn/overturn," applied to the sword) suggests a weapon in perpetual motion, guarding every approach. The LXX renders the cherubim as τοὺς Χερουβιμ and the turning sword as φλογίνην ῥομφαίαν στρεφομένην ("a flaming sword turning"), which matches the Hebrew closely.

The use of the definite article (הַכְּרֻבִים, "the cherubim") in the Hebrew is striking: these are "the cherubim," as though already known to the reader — a sign that the cherub tradition was well-established in the narrative world of the text before its explicit description in Exodus 25 (the cherubim over the Ark). They function as cosmic guardians, and their reappearance in the Tabernacle and Temple architecture links the sanctuary to the original garden.

**16. לִשְׁמֹר אֶת דֶּרֶךְ עֵץ הַחַיִּים (lishmor et derekh ets hachayyim) — "to guard the way to the tree of life"** (v. 24)

The chapter ends on the word life — עֵץ הַחַיִּים, the tree of life, which was first mentioned in 2:9. The expulsion is not a destruction but a sealing: the way to the tree of life is blocked but not abolished. The cherubim and the turning sword guard the way (הַדֶּרֶךְ), and the word derekh ("way, road, path") is the Hebrew word that will carry enormous theological weight throughout the rest of the Scriptures — the "way" of the LORD, the "way" of life, the declaration "I am the way" in later tradition. The tree of life reappears in the final chapters of Revelation (Rev 22:2, 14, 19). The formal translation preserves the Hebrew's understated precision: the way is guarded, not destroyed.

---

### LXX (Vaticanus) Variants — Genesis 3

The Vaticanus manuscript page for Genesis 3 was read directly. The following are the principal variant readings and their effect on this translation.

**3:1 — φρονιμώτατος ("most prudent") vs. עָרוּם ("crafty")**

The LXX renders the serpent's quality with ὁ ὄφις ἦν φρονιμώτατος ("the serpent was most prudent of all"). φρόνιμος is the Greek word for practical wisdom or prudence — the term of high praise in Greek ethics, used in the Septuagint of Joseph's wisdom and in the New Testament of the "wise" virgins (Matt 25). Its superlative φρονιμώτατος applied to the serpent is deeply ironic: the most practically wise of creatures is the one who sets wisdom against obedience to God. This is consistent with the Hebrew but adds a specific Greek philosophical register that enriches the irony. "Crafty" is retained as the English for עָרוּם, but the LXX's use of φρόνιμος is a significant interpretive tradition worth preserving in the notes.

**3:5 — ὡς θεοί ("as gods") vs. כֵּאלֹהִים ("as God" or "as gods")**

As noted above (translation decision 5), Vaticanus reads ὡς θεοί, the plural, "as gods." The MT אֱלֹהִים is morphologically plural and could be taken either way. The LXX translator takes the plural, which is also consistent with the divine council language of v. 22 ("as one of Us"). This is the most significant translational divergence in Genesis 3: the difference between the serpent promising semi-divine status among a plurality of divine beings versus promising identity with the one God. I follow the MT with "as God" (singular referent) while noting the LXX plural as a live alternative.

**3:15 — τηρήσει ("shall watch for") vs. יְשׁוּפְךָ ("shall bruise")**

As noted above (translation decision 10), the LXX renders both instances of שׁוּף with τηρέω ("to watch, to guard, to lie in wait for") — a notably different image from "bruise" or "crush." The LXX reading turns the verse from a combat prediction (striking blows) into a mutual surveillance (each watching for the opportunity to harm the other). This is ancient and attested but departs from the military/physical force imagery of the Hebrew שׁוּף. "Bruise" is retained from the MT.

**3:16 — LXX καὶ πρὸς τὸν ἄνδρα σου ἡ ἀποστροφή σου ("and toward your husband shall be your turning") vs. MT תְּשׁוּקָתֵךְ ("your desire")**

The LXX renders תְּשׁוּקָה as ἀποστροφή, literally "a turning back / a return toward." This is a striking interpretive rendering: the woman's teshuqah is understood not as desire in a longing sense but as a turning or orientation — her whole self turning toward the man. This is neither superior nor inferior to "desire" as a rendering; it captures a different nuance of the same reality. "Desire" is retained as the MT rendering.

**3:20 — Ζωή ("Life") for חַוָּה (Chavvah / Eve)**

As noted above (translation decision 13), Vaticanus renders the name as Ζωή — a translation of the meaning rather than a transliteration of the sound. This is rare in the LXX's treatment of proper names (most are transliterated). The choice of Ζωή shows how deeply the LXX translator heard the etymological connection: Chavvah = life = Zoe, mother of all the living. "Eve" is retained as the established English rendering.

---

### Summary Table — Genesis 3 Manuscript Comparison

| Verse | MT | LXX (Vaticanus) | DSS (4QGen-b) | Translation | Note |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 3:1 | עָרוּם ("crafty") | φρονιμώτατος ("most prudent") | Confirms MT | "crafty" | LXX ironic wisdom tradition noted |
| 3:5 | כֵּאלֹהִים ("as God/gods") | ὡς θεοί ("as gods") | Confirms MT | "as God" | LXX plural alternative noted |
| 3:8 | לְרוּחַ הַיּוֹם ("wind of the day") | τὸ δειλινόν ("in the evening") | Confirms MT | "in the wind of the day" | LXX temporal; MT meteorological |
| 3:15 | יְשׁוּפְךָ ("shall bruise") | τηρήσει ("shall watch for") | Confirms MT | "shall bruise" | LXX surveillance image noted |
| 3:16 | תְּשׁוּקָה ("desire") | ἀποστροφή ("turning") | Confirms MT | "desire" | LXX directional nuance noted |
| 3:20 | חַוָּה (Chavvah) | Ζωή ("Life") | Confirms MT | "Eve" | LXX translates meaning not sound |
| 3:21 | כָּתְנוֹת עוֹר ("garments of skin") | χιτῶνας δερματίνους ("leather tunics") | Confirms MT | "garments of skin" | LXX confirms; no variant |
| 3:24 | הַכְּרֻבִים ("the cherubim") | τοὺς Χερουβιμ | Confirms MT | "the cherubim" | LXX transliterates; no variant |

**Overall assessment:** The DSS (4QGen-b) and Vaticanus do not require any change to the translation text of Genesis 3. The LXX supplies several readings that enrich the interpretive tradition — particularly φρονιμώτατος at v. 1 (the serpent as supremely wise), ὡς θεοί at v. 5 (the divine council implication), and Ζωή at v. 20 (the translated name of Eve). The MT is followed at every point where it diverges from the LXX, with all significant LXX variants documented here. The textual tradition is stable: Genesis 3 in the Leningrad Codex represents the Masoretic tradition faithfully and agrees with the Qumran evidence where available.

---

### Structural and Theological Note

Genesis 3 closes the Eden narrative that began at 2:4 and constitutes what scholars call the second of the major toledot sections of Genesis (the toledot of heaven and earth, 2:4–4:26). It is structurally built around a series of divine interrogations and pronouncements: the serpent questioned (v. 1), the woman corrects (vv. 2–3), the serpent denies (vv. 4–5), the act (v. 6), the shame (v. 7), the hiding (v. 8), the divine questions (vv. 9–13), the curses (vv. 14–19), the naming (v. 20), the clothing (v. 21), the divine council speech (v. 22), and the expulsion (vv. 23–24). The movement is from the garden inward (the tree in the midst, 3:3) to the expulsion outward (the east, 3:24), mirroring the placement of the garden "to the east" in 2:8.

Key terminological continuities with Genesis 1–2 are maintained throughout:
- "the LORD God" (יהוה אֱלֹהִים) continues as in 2:4ff.
- "formed" (יצר) does not appear in this chapter; "made" (עשׂה) appears in 3:1 (beasts the LORD God had made) and 3:21 (garments He made)
- "living" (חַי) in v. 20 echoes "living soul" (נֶפֶשׁ חַיָּה) of 2:7
- "the ground" (אֲדָמָה, adamah) recurs in vv. 17, 19, 23 — the man is returned to the substance from which he was formed

The chapter ends with the cherubim guarding the way to the tree of life. The tree of life, first mentioned in 2:9, is now the goal of a journey that cannot be completed from within. The entire subsequent narrative of Scripture may be read, in one register, as the story of how that way is eventually reopened.

— LOGOS
